cards.front: What is a template non-type parameter?
cards.back: A template parameter where the type is predefined and is substituted for a constexpr value passed in as an argument.

cards.text: To create a template class with a type parameter T and an integer non-type parameter: {{c1::template <typename T, int size>}}

cards.front: What types can be used as template non-type parameters?
cards.back:
Allowed types:
  • Integral types
  • Enumeration types
  • Pointer or reference to class object
  • Pointer or reference to function
  • Pointer or reference to class member function
  • std::nullptr_t
  • Floating point type (since C++20)

cards.text: The syntax {{c1::template <typename T, int size>}} declares a template with {{c2::one type parameter (T)}} and {{c3::one non-type parameter (size)}}.

cards.question: What does this code declare?
cards.answer: A template class StaticArray with a type parameter T and a non-type parameter size that determines the array size at compile time.
cards.explanation: The non-type parameter size must be a constexpr value, allowing the array to be allocated statically without dynamic memory.

cards.front: How does std::array use template non-type parameters?
cards.back: In std::array<int, 5>, int is a type parameter and 5 is a non-type parameter that specifies the array size.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Compilation error: x is not constexpr. Template non-type arguments must be constexpr values.
cards.explanation: Template non-type parameters require compile-time constant values. Use constexpr int x{ 4 }; or pass a literal like Foo<4>.

cards.text: Template non-type parameters must be {{c1::constexpr}} values, meaning they must be known at {{c2::compile time}}.

cards.front: Why can StaticArray<int, 12> allocate m_array statically without dynamic memory?
cards.back: Because the size (12) is a constexpr value known at compile time, so the compiler knows the exact array size and can allocate it on the stack.

cards.text: When you instantiate StaticArray<int, 12>, the compiler replaces {{c1::size with 12}}, making m_array of type {{c2::int[12]}}.

cards.concept: When to use template non-type parameters
cards.explanation:
Use template non-type parameters when you need compile-time constant values that affect class/function behavior, such as:
  • Array sizes for static allocation
  • Buffer sizes
  • Compile-time configuration values
examples: template <typename T, int N> class Array { T data[N]; };
std::array<int, 10> arr;  // Size is non-type parameter

cards.text: In std::array<int, 5>, the type parameter is {{c1::int}} and the non-type parameter is {{c2::5}}.
Floating point types can be used as non-type parameters {{c1::since C++20}}.
